docs.title: ⚡ Skills & Learning Roadmap
docs.text: Here is the tech stack I am actively mastering right now:

docs.title: 💻 Programming & Systems
docs.text:
• C / C++: For core embedded architectures, speed-critical programs, and deep hardware control.
  • Rust: For building secure, high-performance web backends and memory-safe system utilities.
  • Python: For swift scripting, network testing scripts, and building machine learning prototypes.

docs.title: 🛡️ Cyber Security & Networks
docs.text:

  • Ethical Hacking: Recreating simulated network penetrations in breach labs and resolving complex binary challenges.
  • Secure Engineering: Building codebase constructs designed to deflect injection and memory leaks from day one.

docs.title: 🤝 Join the Journey
docs.text:
I believe in building and learning in public. Let's create something great together:

  • Contribute & Correct: Spot a bug or typo? Click the "Edit this page" button at the top right of any page to suggest improvements.
  • Reach Out: Connect with me directly on my GitHub Profile or find me posting regular tech updates on LinkedIn.

docs.title: 👤 About Me
docs.text:
Ajay Elumalai

Electronics & Communication Engineering Student @ Rajalakshmi Institute of Technology, Chennai

💻 Focus: Systems, Security, IoT 🛠️ Learning: CTFs & Rust 📍 Location: Chennai, India

"இரவும் பகலும் இதயம் வழியும்,வழியும் கனவு இதழை அடையும்."

— Hiphop Tamizha

Hi, I am Ajay! I am currently studying Electronics and Communication Engineering (ECE). I live at the intersection of physical hardware circuits and the vast world of software engineering and cybersecurity.

I am a relentless explorer who loves diving into new technologies. Currently, my journey has led me deep into solving Cybersecurity CTF challenges and mastering systems programming with Rust.

⚡ Core Passions & Hobbies
  • 🔌 ECE & Hardware: Building and programming microcontrollers (STM32 & ESP32), designing digital circuits, and exploring bare-metal firmware.
  • 💻 Software Engineering: Crafting modern web portals, multi-tier mobile systems, and quantum-inspired programming compilers.
  • 🛡️ Cybersecurity: Participating in Capture The Flag (CTF) security contests, auditing codebases for safety, and understanding network penetrations.
🛠️ Featured Projects & Innovation Hub

Here is a curated showcase of my top innovations. Slide your mouse over each item and tap anywhere on the card to open its dedicated deep dive blog post!

01 🌀 Zyay Language

A highly innovative, Quantum-Inspired Programming Language where variables hold probability wave spaces and functions run as parallel competing paths.

Python Compiler C Runtime Quantum Model 02 🌐 Club Hub (JDBF)

A Next-Generation Decentralized Campus Community Hub scaling real-time event QR check-ins, leaderboards, and administrative command boards.

Next.js 15 Supabase Postgres RLS 03 🅿️ Spotsy Captain & User

A complete parking-booking platform featuring dual cross-platform Flutter applications powered by a secure WebSocket-supported Spring Boot backend.

Flutter Spring Boot WebSockets 04 🏫 IMS (College Management)

An institutional student and faculty profile registry system custom-tailored for the administrative structure of RIT, Chennai.

React / Vite Python API SQLite 🤝 Let's Connect!

I would love to collaborate on exciting hardware or software projects. Keep track of my commits, or get in touch:

  • GitHub: @ajax-y
  • LinkedIn: ajay_elumalai

docs.title: Blog
docs.text:
Welcome to my personal blog! Here you will find deep-dive tutorials, walkthroughs, project post-mortems, and articles spanning ECE, Systems Programming, and Cyber Security.

docs.title: Building GFG Club Hub: The Decentralized Campus Node 🌐
docs.text:
Campus organization is often a chaotic mess of scattered Google Forms, unorganized WhatsApp groups, and spreadsheets that are permanently out of sync. To resolve this manual workload once and for all, my team Dash Dominators built Club Hub—a next-generation decentralized campus event, project registry, and leaderboard ecosystem for GeeksforGeeks campus club.

In this deep dive, we will explore the real-time node architecture, database schema, frontend styling system, and security design that made Club Hub the winning campus scaling engine at our GeeksforGeeks hackathon.

docs.title:
📁ajax-y/jdbf

Club Hub: Next-generation real-time campus node ecosystem built using Next.js 15, Supabase Postgres, Tailwind, and Framer Motion.

View on GitHub ➔
💡 The Core Vision: An Interactive Campus Engine
docs.text:
Club Hub operates on a physical-meets-digital "Node" concept. Rather than functioning as a boring administrative records system, it acts as a living hub built on five core pillars:

                  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │          CLUB HUB EVENT NODE            │
                  └────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                               │
         ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐
         ▼                     ▼                     ▼
┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────┐
│ Live QR Ingress │   │ Hall of Fame    │   │ Live Wall Stream│
│ Merit Points    │   │ Bronze -> Diam. │   │ Projector UI    │
└─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘   └─────────────────┘
  1. The Live Node Scanner (Ingress): Secure QR-based check-in system. Upon successful scan, members instantly receive 10 Merit Points, updating their global rank dynamically.
  2. The Innovation Gallery (Projects): A decentralized home for every club project, supporting real-time likes, feedback comments, and tech stack sorting tags.
  3. Real-Time Hall of Fame (Leaderboards): A global scrolling feed displaying all student activity, with automatic tier progressions (Bronze → Silver → Gold → Diamond).
  4. The Live Wall (Projector View): A high-performance broadcast layout for organizers to project onto walls, displaying real-time attendance graphs and participant cards as they scan in.
  5. Administrative Control Board: Toggles event nodes active/inactive, sends global system notifications, and generates event entry tokens.

docs.title: 🛠️ The Architecture & Technology Stack
docs.text:
To achieve instant, low-latency updates for large student crowds, we chose premium, industry-standard tools:

Technical Layer Choice Key Rationale Frontend Framework Next.js 15+ (App Router) For lightning-fast page loading speeds and highly structured routing patterns. Type Safety TypeScript Ensuring type safety and reliable communication between the client and database. Database Engine PostgreSQL (Supabase) Offers robust relational data mapping, low-latency queries, and native WebSocket triggers. Theme & Layout Tailwind CSS Custom styling system using matrix-inspired cyber-slate dark elements. Micro-Animations Framer Motion Controls layout transitions, glowing gradient headers, and sparkling canvas tails.

docs.title: 🗄️ Database Design & Real-Time Sync Logic
docs.text:
At the core of Club Hub's real-time updating dashboard is the relational Supabase PostgreSQL engine. Here is a simplified look at the database tables we designed:

-- Profiles table tracking student merit points and global rank tiers
CREATE TABLE profiles (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY REFERENCES auth.users,
  full_name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  merit_points INT DEFAULT 0 CHECK (merit_points >= 0),
  tier VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'Bronze',
  avatar_url TEXT,
  updated_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT TIMEZONE('utc'::text, NOW())
);

-- Event Nodes table tracking individual check-in systems
CREATE TABLE event_nodes (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  event_name VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL,
  node_secret VARCHAR(64) UNIQUE NOT NULL, -- QR verification hash
  is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
  capacity INT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT TIMEZONE('utc'::text, NOW())
);

-- Attendance ledger connecting Profiles to Event Nodes
CREATE TABLE event_ingress (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  profile_id UUID REFERENCES profiles(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  node_id UUID REFERENCES event_nodes(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  scanned_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT TIMEZONE('utc'::text, NOW()),
  UNIQUE(profile_id, node_id)
);

docs.title: The Real-Time Synchronization Flow
docs.text:
Instead of polling the server every few seconds (which drains device battery and slows servers), we set up standard PostgreSQL triggers inside Supabase that broadcast updates over WebSockets:

Student Scans QR Code
  ├─► Inserts Row to 'event_ingress'
  ├─► Postgres Trigger Fires
  │     ├─► Adds 10 Merit Points to Student's 'profiles' row
  │     └─► Broadcasts 'NEW_INGRESS' payload via WebSockets
  └─► Live Wall Projector UI catches event and updates dynamically!

docs.title: 🎨 Premium Visual Elements & Tailwind Configurations
docs.text: To give Club Hub an immersive gaming-inspired look, we developed a detailed design system: - Glassmorphism CSS: Semi-transparent cards utilizing Tailwind's backdrop-blur-md and bg-slate-900/80 overlays. - Sparkle Engine: A lightweight HTML5 canvas tracker that spawns particles following your mouse pointer across active dashboards. - Dynamic Leaderboard Scroll: A vertical scrolling list styled to slide newly calculated entries up smoothly using CSS Keyframe animations.

docs.title: 🚦 Getting Started Locally
docs.text:
1. Clone the Repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/ajax-y/jdbf.git
cd jdbf
  2. Environment Variables (.env.local): 
    NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=your_supabase_url_placeholder
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=your_supabase_anonymous_key_placeholder
  3. Database Migration: Apply the provided supabase_setup.sql script inside your Supabase dashboard SQL editor to create the required profiles, nodes, and triggers.
  4. Start the Local Server: 
    npm install
npm run dev

Club Hub shows how standard administrative campus applications can be elevated into premium, highly interactive systems by combining fast databases with beautiful frontends!

docs.title: Secure Coding in Hardware Systems: Bridging the Gap
docs.text:
When we think of cyber security vulnerabilities, our minds immediately jump to web application firewalls, SQL injections, or cloud configuration slip-ups. But as an Electronics and Communication Engineering (ECE) student, I’ve realized that some of the most critical vulnerabilities lurk much closer to the physical silicon.

In this inaugural blog post, we'll dive into why secure hardware coding matters, how C/C++ memory vulnerabilities manifest on microcontrollers, and how memory-safe options like Rust are changing the landscape.

docs.title: The Core Challenge: Memory Constraints vs. Security
docs.text:
Embedded microcontrollers (like STM32 chips or ESP32 modules) have extremely limited resources compared to modern servers. They often operate with kilobytes of RAM and no operating system (bare-metal) or light Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS).

To conserve space and keep performance lightning-fast, safety boundaries are typically stripped away. If you write outside a buffer in a bare-metal C program, there is no Operating System to throw a Segmentation Fault. The microcontroller will happily overwrite adjacent configuration registers, leading to unpredictable system behavior—or worse, remote code execution.

docs.title: A Classic MCU Buffer Overflow Example
docs.text:
Consider this innocent-looking packet receiver code:

#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>

#define BUFFER_SIZE 64

void process_packet(char *raw_payload) {
    char local_buffer[BUFFER_SIZE];

    // UNSAFE: Overwrites local_buffer if payload exceeds 64 bytes
    strcpy(local_buffer, raw_payload);

    printf("Processing: %s\n", local_buffer);
}

If an attacker injects a telemetry stream payload exceeding 64 bytes, they can overwrite the function's return address on the stack. On a connected IoT home thermostat or grid controller, this could allow full control over the appliance.

[!WARNING] Always enforce bounds checking on embedded input interfaces (UART, SPI, Wi-Fi buffers) using safe functions like strncpy or custom bounds assertions.

docs.title: ⚡ The Modern Alternative: Rust for Embedded Systems
docs.text:
One of the most exciting trends is the adoption of Rust for embedded systems development (no_std).

Rust enforces memory safety at compile-time without a garbage collector, giving developers C-like performance with zero-cost abstractions:

// A safe Rust equivalent
fn process_packet(raw_payload: &[u8]) {
    let mut local_buffer = [0u8; 64];

    if raw_payload.len() > local_buffer.len() {
        println!("Error: Packet size exceeds maximum limit!");
        return;
    }

    local_buffer[..raw_payload.len()].copy_from_slice(raw_payload);
}

If you try to copy a slice larger than the buffer size without checking, Rust raises a compiler error or panics safely instead of silently corrupting RAM.

docs.title: 🤝 What's Next?
docs.text:
Over the coming weeks, I’ll be sharing walkthroughs of: 1. BreachLab Walkthroughs: Recreating security exploits and patching them. 2. STM32 Bare-Metal Drivers: Writing custom, robust drivers in pure C. 3. Cyber Security CTF write-ups: Breakdown of challenges and methodology.

Are you working on embedded systems or learning ethical hacking? I’d love to hear your thoughts. Leave a comment below or connect with me via GitHub.

docs.title: Designing IMS: An Administrative Command Center for RIT, Chennai 🏫
docs.text:
Efficient academic operations require robust tracking of student enrollment databases, semester schedules, faculty course maps, and department limits. To move away from manual paperwork and messy excel lists, I built IMS (Institutional Management System)—a clean, lightweight student and staff record registry custom-tailored for the administrative structure of Rajalakshmi Institute of Technology (RIT), Chennai.

In this article, we will examine the system architecture, SQLite relational database structure, and the Vite React admin interface.

docs.title:
📁ajax-y/ims

IMS: A collegiate profile, course enrollment, and directory dashboard built using React Vite, Python, and serverless SQLite database structures.

View on GitHub ➔
💡 What is IMS?
docs.text:
IMS is designed as a centralized record dashboard for university administrators. It provides separate views and management boards to perform daily institutional tracking:

                            ┌────────────────────────┐
                            │    IMS ADMIN PORTAL    │
                            └───────────┬────────────┘
                                        │
             ┌──────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────┐
             ▼                                                     ▼
┌────────────────────────┐                            ┌────────────────────────┐
│ Student Database       │                            │ Faculty Directory      │
│ - Course Enrollments   │                            │ - Departmental Mapping │
│ - Grade Tracking       │                            │ - Schedule Rosters     │
└────────────────────────┘                            └────────────────────────┘
  • Student Registry Panel: Manage academic profiles, handle student enrollments, record major/minor divisions, and track marks.
  • Faculty Roster Management: Monitor active academic professors, map their specialized courses, and coordinate departmental teaching hours.
  • Security Control Center: A secure admin credentials checkpoint ensuring that sensitive academic lists cannot be modified without permission.

docs.title: 🛠️ Lightweight Architecture Blueprint
docs.text:
To ensure the system is easy to run on regular admin computers without complex cloud environments, the architecture was designed to be lightweight:

  • Vite React Frontend: Renders a fast, responsive dashboard using structured admin panels.
  • Python (FastAPI / Flask logic): Powers the backend, handling queries, authenticating sessions, and serving database payloads.
  • SQLite Relational Database (ims.db): A serverless SQL database engine. The entire database is stored as a single file on the local computer, meaning zero database server setup time and exceptionally simple backup creation.

docs.title: 🗄️ Database Design (SQLite)
docs.text:
The backend interacts with a relational database layout mapping students, faculty, and academic departments:

-- Departments Table
CREATE TABLE departments (
  dept_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  dept_name VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  dept_code VARCHAR(10) UNIQUE NOT NULL
);

-- Students Table tracking academic statuses
CREATE TABLE students (
  student_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  enrollment_no VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  last_name VARCHAR(50),
  email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
  dept_id INTEGER,
  current_semester INTEGER DEFAULT 1,
  academic_status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'ACTIVE',
  FOREIGN KEY(dept_id) REFERENCES departments(dept_id)
);

-- Faculty directory and academic titles
CREATE TABLE faculty (
  faculty_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  employee_code VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  last_name VARCHAR(50),
  email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
  dept_id INTEGER,
  designation VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  FOREIGN KEY(dept_id) REFERENCES departments(dept_id)
);

-- Course registries connected to teachers
CREATE TABLE courses (
  course_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
  course_code VARCHAR(15) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  course_name VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL,
  credits INTEGER NOT NULL CHECK (credits > 0),
  faculty_id INTEGER,
  FOREIGN KEY(faculty_id) REFERENCES faculty(faculty_id)
);

docs.title: 🎨 The Vite React Admin Experience
docs.text: To give admins a beautiful, distraction-free work environment, the frontend was designed to be clean and easy to use: * Search Filters: Type-ahead filters on the Student and Faculty panels let admins instantly find entries using names, registration numbers, or departments. * Status Indicators: Color-coded status markers quickly show key information (e.g., active students are shown in green, while graduated profiles are marked in soft blue). * Data Forms: Clean, interactive modal forms handle all data entry with built-in validation checks, preventing typos or missing details.

docs.title: 🚦 How to Launch IMS Locally
docs.text:
Setting up the project on an administrative computer is straightforward:

  1. Clone the Repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/ajax-y/ims.git
cd ims
  2. Launch the Backend Logic: 
    python main.py
  3. Start the Vite Frontend Panel: 
    npm install
npm run dev

IMS demonstrates how simple Python utilities and lightweight relational databases can be turned into highly useful tools for schools and universities by adding clean, user-friendly frontend dashboards!

docs.title: Designing Spotsy: A Real-Time Parking Space Booking Ecosystem 🅿️
docs.text:
In fast-growing metropolitan centers, finding parking is one of the most frustrating bottlenecks of daily commuting. Drivers waste fuel and crowd city roads driving in circles. To solve this challenge, I designed Spotsy—a high-performance, real-time parking spot reservation ecosystem inspired by modern ride-hailing services.

In this comprehensive architecture deep dive, we will explore the dual-app Flutter configuration, the Spring Boot WebSocket backend, PostgreSQL table schema models, and secure BillDesk payment integrations.

📁spotsy-captain

Owner app built with Flutter and Riverpod. Manage listings, accept incoming parking reservations, and track active earnings.

📁spotsy-user

Driver app built with Flutter and OpenStreetMap. Scan nearby spaces, filter EV charge points, reserve, check out, and pay.

docs.title: 🏗️ The System Architecture: Three-Tier Blueprint
docs.text:
Spotsy operates as a complete three-tier platform consisting of two separate mobile apps communicating with a shared backend server:

┌────────────────────────┐         ┌────────────────────────┐
│  Spotsy User App       │         │  Spotsy Captain App    │
│  (Flutter - Driver)    │         │  (Flutter - Space Owner)│
└───────────┬────────────┘         └───────────┬────────────┘
            │                                  │
            └─────────► [ WebSockets & REST ] ◄┘
                                │
                                ▼
                   ┌────────────────────────┐
                   │   Spring Boot Backend  │
                   │   & PostgreSQL DB      │
                   └────────────────────────┘
  1. Spotsy User App (Flutter): For drivers to scan, filter, reserve, pay, and navigate to parking slots.
  2. Spotsy Captain App (Flutter): For space owners to list driveways or commercial spaces, approve requests, and manage earnings.
  3. Spotsy Backend (Spring Boot 3.x + PostgreSQL): Manages user session tokens, processes real-time WebSocket payloads, and processes payment gateways.

docs.title: 🛠️ The Technical Stack At a Glance
docs.text: Layer Component Technical Role Mobile Client Flutter 3.x Controls multi-platform Android, iOS, and Web deployment. State Management Riverpod Ensures robust, reactive, and easily testable state maps across apps. Map Rendering OpenStreetMap Native maps rendering via flutter_map (fully free & open-source). Geolocation geolocator + location Accurately streams user latitude and longitude GPS arrays. Backend Framework Spring Boot 3.x Powering high-performance Java REST APIs & STOMP WebSockets. Relational Database PostgreSQL Stores profiles, vehicles, and payment ledgers securely.

docs.title: 🗄️ Database Schema Design (PostgreSQL models)
docs.text:
To support secure financial transactions, KYC storage, and active booking queues, we designed a highly robust database layout:

-- Main Users Table holding security details and KYC verification states
CREATE TABLE users (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  phone_number VARCHAR(15) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
  first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
  last_name VARCHAR(50),
  aadhar_number_encrypted TEXT, -- Stored securely under AES-256
  kyc_status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'PENDING',
  created_at TIMESTAMP WITHOUT TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- User registered vehicles mapping Ev support
CREATE TABLE user_vehicles (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  vehicle_type VARCHAR(10) CHECK (vehicle_type IN ('BIKE', 'CAR', 'SUV')),
  registration_number VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
  vehicle_name VARCHAR(50),
  is_ev BOOLEAN DEFAULT false
);

-- Parking Space Registries mapped to Captains
CREATE TABLE parking_spaces (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  owner_id UUID REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  space_name VARCHAR(150) NOT NULL,
  address TEXT NOT NULL,
  latitude DECIMAL(10, 8) NOT NULL,
  longitude DECIMAL(11, 8) NOT NULL,
  total_spaces INT NOT NULL,
  available_spaces INT NOT NULL,
  price_per_hour DECIMAL(10, 2) NOT NULL,
  has_ev_charging BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
  is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true
);

-- Bookings ledger mapping transaction values
CREATE TABLE bookings (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id),
  parking_space_id UUID REFERENCES parking_spaces(id),
  vehicle_id UUID REFERENCES user_vehicles(id),
  booking_status VARCHAR(40) DEFAULT 'PENDING_OWNER_CONFIRMATION',
  check_in_time TIMESTAMP,
  check_out_time TIMESTAMP,
  total_amount DECIMAL(10, 2),
  payment_status VARCHAR(20) DEFAULT 'PENDING'
);

docs.title: 🔄 The Real-Time Booking & Location Loop
docs.text: Rather than relying on classic refresh screens, Spotsy manages user journeys using full-duplex WebSockets.

docs.title: 1. The Booking Request Flow
docs.text:
User App                           Spring Boot API                     Captain App
   │                                     │                                  │
   ├─► Sends WebSockets BOOKING ────────►│                                  │
   │   (/app/booking/request)            ├─► Push STOMP notification ──────►│
   │                                     │   (/topic/owner/bookings)        │
   │                                     │                                  │
   │                                     │◄─ Owner Taps ACCEPT ─────────────┤
   │                                     │   (/app/booking/accept)          │
   │◄─ Confirmed Toast Received ─────────┤                                  │
   │   (/topic/user/bookings)            │                                  │

docs.title: 2. Live Driver Location Tracking
docs.text:
Once a booking is confirmed, the driver’s app starts streaming raw GPS coordinates: - Driver -> STOMP Channel: /app/booking/{id}/location - Spring Boot -> Captain Topic: /topic/booking/{id}/user-location

This allows the Captain (space owner) to look at their dashboard map and see the driver's real-time vehicle movement approaching the garage!

docs.title: 💳 Secure BillDesk Payment Integration Flow
docs.text:
To ensure secure checkout, we integrated a full digital transaction model:

  1. Initiation: When the Captain approves the booking request, the driver taps "Proceed to Payment". The client calls the REST endpoint: POST /api/bookings/{id}/payment
  2. Order Generation: The Spring Boot backend connects to BillDesk APIs and returns a cryptographic checkout token.
  3. Gateway Checkout: The mobile app opens the integrated web-view overlay allowing the user to pay safely using cards, UPI, or wallets.
  4. Webhook Handshake: Upon completion, BillDesk notifies our backend at: POST /api/payments/webhook
  5. Validation: The backend validates the payment signature, marks the booking as COMPLETED, updates the Captain's ledger, and triggers a real-time WebSocket toast back to the mobile app.

docs.title: 🛡️ Critical Security Configurations
docs.text:
Spotsy prioritizes data protection at every level of the database and transit systems: * Encrypted Aadhaar & Bank Details: Raw personal identification numbers are encrypted under AES-256 standard encryption keys before saving to PostgreSQL. * JWT Security Lifecycles: REST endpoints are protected with standard JSON Web Tokens. Access tokens expire after 15 minutes, with highly secure Refresh tokens running a 7-day slide cycle.

Spotsy demonstrates how combining robust mobile frontends with modern reactive backend ecosystems completely transforms regular urban commuting!

docs.title: Designing Zyay: A Quantum-Inspired Programming Language 🌀
docs.text:
What if your programming language didn't just execute line-by-line in a predictable way, but instead worked like quantum physics? What if variables held a superposition of states, and running code explored multiple paths simultaneously before collapsing to the best answer?

This is Zyay (pronounced Zee-aye), a quantum-inspired language I built from scratch. In this article, we'll dive deep into the formal specifications, compiler internals, runtime arena memory managers, and unique paradigms that make this language unlike anything else.

docs.title:
📁ajax-y/zyay

A Quantum‑inspired programming language with parallel wave superposition paths, variable entanglement, error tunneling, and arena allocators.

View on GitHub ➔
💡 The Core Philosophy: "Intent over Procedure"
docs.text:
In classic programming languages (like Python, C, or Java), you write precise, sequential instructions telling the CPU exactly how to find an answer. You manage the loops, condition blocks, and storage registers.

In Zyay, the paradigm shifts. The programmer defines intent (what they want the outcome to look like) and constructs superpositions (all possible values a variable could hold). The language runtime then executes parallel paths, scoring each potential solution before executing a measurement collapse to return the single best result.

docs.title: ⚡ Unique Quantum Constructs in Zyay
docs.text:
Zyay implements several fundamental principles of quantum mechanics directly as language keywords:

Keyword Quantum Counterpart What it accomplishes wave Superposition Spawns a parallel search space across an iterable range or collection. entangle Entanglement Locks two variables together so they share a single physical memory address. prune Wave Interference Discards a branch or halts execution path when constraints are violated. tunnel Quantum Tunneling Allows operations to pass safely through runtime signals (like Division-by-Zero). amplify Phase Amplitude Adjusts the probability weight or score of the active execution path. observe Measurement Captures a state inside a wave loop; terminates the wave and collapses to the highest-scored path.

docs.title: 1. Superposition & Wave Collapsing (wave & observe)
docs.text:
A wave statement doesn't loop sequentially. It places the iterator into a superposition of all possible indices. When compilation targets multi-threaded execution, these branches execute in parallel paths:

// Create a superposition across numbers 0 through 9
wave i in range(0, 10):
    // Inside the wave, we test a criteria
    if i % 2 == 0:
        amplify(2.0) // Double the score weight of even numbers

    observe(i * i) // Record the squared result for this path
When the wave finishes, it automatically collapses and outputs only the highest-scored observed item to the user.

docs.title: 2. Quantum Entanglement (entangle)
docs.text:
In Zyay, the entangle keyword links two variable declarations. They do not merely point to the same object reference; their physical underlying memory addresses are permanently tied together at runtime:

var x = 10
var y = 20

entangle x <=> y

x = 99
print(y) // Prints 99! Both share the exact same physical slot.

docs.title: 3. Error Tunneling (tunnel)
docs.text:
In standard programming, dividing by zero causes the Operating System to trigger a hardware interrupt (SIGFPE), immediately crashing your application with a Core Dump. In Zyay, we can use tunneling to safely pass through the barrier:

var result = 0
tunnel arithmetic_error:
    result = 100 / 0 // Usually crashes

print(result) // Prints 0 (or default state) instead of crashing!

docs.title: 🛠️ Deep Dive into the Compiler Internals
docs.text:
The Zyay Compiler (zyayc) is built fully from scratch in Python with zero external dependencies, rendering it lightweight and fast. It compiles your code using a multi-phase system:

graph LR
    Source[.zy Source] --> Lexer[Lexer]
    Lexer --> Parser[Parser]
    Parser --> Validator[Semantic Validator]
    Validator --> Transpiler[Transpiler]
    Transpiler --> CSource[C Code & Runtime]
    CSource --> GCC[GCC Compiler]
    GCC --> Native[Binary Executable]

docs.title: Phase 1: The Tokenizing Lexer (lexer.py)
docs.text: The lexer reads the raw text, ignores comments, tracks Python-style indentations and dedents, and groups characters into typed Token structures (like TOKEN_KEYWORD, TOKEN_IDENTIFIER, TOKEN_LITERAL).

docs.title: Phase 2: The AST Parser (parser.py)
docs.text: Using a recursive-descent parsing strategy, the parser converts the flat list of tokens into a highly structured Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). Each node represents a structural operation (like a variable assignment, a wave loop, or a logic branch).

docs.title: Phase 3: Semantic Validator (validator.py)
docs.text: The validator enforces compiler safety before writing code. It audits for type mismatches, variable scopes, and warns you with custom errors: - E011 (Type Mismatch) - E031 (Division By Zero check) - E052 (Self-Entanglement check)

docs.title: Phase 4: The Transpiler & C Runtime (transpiler.py & runtime/)
docs.text: The transpiler translates the validated AST map directly into standard ANSI C. It links in our custom C Runtime headers (zyay_runtime.h), which implements: * The Arena Memory Allocator: Rather than calling standard malloc and free for every heap operation (which slows execution and fragments memory), Zyay allocates memory in big chunks (Arenas). When a wave path is pruned or terminates, the entire arena is wiped in one operation, giving zero-overhead garbage collection. * Multi-threading: Leverages standard C pthread libraries to execute wave paths in parallel across CPU cores.

docs.title: 🚦 How to Build and Run Your Own Language
docs.text:
The Zyay compiler is ready for compilation on Linux, macOS, or Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL).

  1. Clone the Repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/ajax-y/zyay.git
cd zyay
  2. Write a Program (hello.zy): 
    print("Hello from the Zyay Quantum space!")
  3. Compile and Run: 
    python3 -m zyayc.zyayc hello.zy
./hello

Zyay demonstrates that programming languages don't have to be rigid or traditional. By experimenting with quantum mechanical models, we can explore innovative ways to build memory-safe, parallel, and robust software architectures!

docs.title: 🛡️ Cyber Security & Linux Learning Logs
docs.text: Welcome to my cyber security notes! This page tracks my hands-on practice with CTF (Capture The Flag) platforms, security tools, and the Linux commands I discover through research and troubleshooting.

docs.title: 🎯 Current Platforms I Am Using
docs.text:
• BreachLab: Practical network labs and system exploitation.
  • OverTheWire: Practising Linux terminal basics and wargames (Bandit).
  • CyLab Security: Solving structured lab challenges and web vulnerabilities.
  • Google & Write-ups: Independent research for troubleshooting stuck labs.

docs.title: 🐚 Linux Commands I've Mastered
docs.text: Here are the essential commands I learned while solving labs and exploring the system on my own:

docs.title: 📁 File & Directory Navigation
docs.text:
• pwd: Prints the current working directory path.
  • ls -la: Lists all files, including hidden ones, with details like permissions.
  • cd: Changes directories. Use cd .. to go back one step.

docs.title: 🔍 Searching & Filtering Text
docs.text:
• cat: Displays the full content of a file in the terminal.
  • grep: Searches for specific keywords inside files or output.
  • find / -name "flag.txt": Searches the entire system for a specific file.

docs.title: 🛡️ Permissions & System Info
docs.text:
• chmod: Changes file permissions (e.g., chmod +x script.sh to make it runnable).
  • whoami: Displays the username of the current active session.
  • ssh username@host: Connects securely to remote CTF servers (like OverTheWire).

docs.title: 🚀 Next Steps on My Radar
docs.text:
1. Complete OverTheWire Bandit up to Level 15 to solidify my Linux core.
  2. Learn how to use Nmap on BreachLab to scan for open network ports.
  3. Keep updating this file every time I learn a new terminal shortcut.

docs.title: 🛡️ Cyber Security for Beginners
docs.text:
Welcome to the Cyber Security zone! If you've ever wondered how hackers break into systems, or how we protect digital assets, you are in the right place.

This guide is designed for absolute beginners to help you understand the core concepts of cybersecurity without any complex jargon.

docs.title: 🏛️ The Three Pillars of Security: The CIA Triad
docs.text:
Every security professional in the world uses a concept called the CIA Triad to protect information. It has nothing to do with the intelligence agency! It stands for:

Pillar What It Means Simple Example Confidentiality Keeping secrets secret. Only allowed people can read the data. Your private WhatsApp messages are encrypted so only you and your friend can read them. Integrity Making sure data is not changed or tampered with. When you transfer money, the banking system makes sure the amount stays the same. Availability Making sure systems are up and working when needed. Your student portal or email service should load quickly whenever you need it.

docs.title: ⚠️ Top 3 Common Security Threats
docs.text: Here are the most common ways systems get compromised:

docs.title: 1. Phishing & Social Engineering (Tricking People)
docs.text: Instead of hacking a computer, hackers often hack the human. They send fake emails pretending to be Google, Netflix, or your bank, asking you to click a link and log in to steal your password.

docs.title: 2. Broken & Weak Passwords
docs.text: Using simple passwords like 123456, password, or your pet's name makes it incredibly easy for automated hacking software to guess your password in seconds.

docs.title: 3. Software Vulnerabilities (Code Bugs)
docs.text: When programmers write software, they sometimes make mistakes. Security researchers look for these mistakes (bugs) and fix them before bad actors can exploit them to crash the system or steal data.

docs.title: 💡 Quick Tips: How to Protect Yourself Today
docs.text:
Here are three simple habits that instantly make you more secure:

[!TIP] Use a Password Manager: Don't reuse passwords! Use a password manager (like Bitwarden or 1Password) to create and store unique, complex passwords for every single site.

[!IMPORTANT] Enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA): Always turn on 2FA (like receiving an SMS code or using an authenticator app) on your email and social accounts. Even if a hacker gets your password, they still can't log in!

[!NOTE] Keep Software Updated: Whenever your phone or computer asks to update, do it! Most updates include patches for security vulnerabilities that keep you safe.

docs.title: What is Breach Lab
docs.text:
Breach Lab is a website where peoples can learn Cyber Security by doing CTF (Capture The Flag).

  • Breach Lab is a persistent server with real vulnerabilities Linux boxes you SSH into and root it.
  • Free, No Signup, No Paywall, No Certificate.

docs.title: What is not Breach Lab
docs.text:
• Not a Multiple choice questions.
  • Not a curriculum, not a course, not a bootcamp.
  • Flags here are real shell passwords, in real machine.

docs.title: Who this for
docs.text:
Operators. People who would rather get stuck for six hours and figure something out than be told the answer in three minutes. People who understand that the only way you actually learn this craft is by doing it on systems that fight back.

If you're tired of multiple choice quizzes and want to learn the way attackers actually learn, there's a server waiting

Start Here

docs.title: Ghost L0 (Level 0):
docs.text:
First when I entered to this site I have seen a starting point, which is Ghost L0.

In this Level they gave some data.

docs.title: SSH access:
docs.text: Host: 204.168.229.209 Post: 2222 User: ghost0 Password: ghost0

docs.title: Procedure:
docs.text:
By seeing this I thought I need to login to that IP and Enter User name and Password as given.

So I opened i the IP in the browser, but the website not opened. So I googled it like How can I login to the IP address? and I found that I need to use SSH to login to the IP address.

So the command to login to the given IP address is,

ssh ghost0@204.168.229.209 -p 2222

Then they asked for the password, I entered the password ghost0.

Now it opened. I logged in to the machine. After I entered to the machine I saw that, I am in the home directory of ghost0. Then I tried to execute some linux commands like ls, ls -a, pwd. So I found some files notable ones are README, and a directory workspace. Now I wanted to see the contents in the README file. So I use cat command to see the content of the file.

cat README
The output of the command is,

ANALYST WORKSTATION — KAEL
Last active: 2026-03-28 02:47 UTC
Status: Abandoned

If you're reading this, you found my terminal.
I left in a hurry. Didn't have time to clean up.

Nothing in this shell is hidden. It's just here.
If you can't see it, you're not looking hard enough.

Don't leave traces.

— KAEL

So I found out there is no useful information in this file. So I moved to workspace directory and then use ls -a command to see all the file and directory in that directory. I seen on file a file notes.txt and a directory archive. First I used used cat command in the file named notes.txt to see the contents of the file.

cat notes.txt
The output of the command is,

OPERATIONAL NOTES — KAEL
========================
Target: internal network segment 10.4.x.x
Method: passive recon, no active scanning
Status: ongoing

Credentials filed separately in archive/.
Do not store passwords in plaintext notes.

So here also no credentials or flag. So now I moved to archive directory and then use ls -a command to see all the file and directory in that directory.I seen just only one file named credentials. Now I used cat command to see the content of the file.

cat credentials

docs.title: Password:
docs.text:
I got the flag to move to next round.

W3lc0m3T0Gh0st

Next Ghost L1

docs.title: SHH ACCESS
docs.text: Host: 204.168.229.209 Port: 2222 User: ghost1 Password: W3lc0m3T0Gh0st (Which we got from last level)

docs.title: Procedure:
docs.text:
Now I login to the machine using that IP and Password which we got from previous level.

ssh ghost1@204.168.229.209 -p 2222

Now I logged in to the machine. First I used ls -a command to see all the files and directory in the machine. I seen some files namely MANIFEST and 'file name'. I used cat command to content in the MANIFEST file.

cat MANIFEST

And seen some text message in it.

NOTES — KAEL
────────────
I named my files to watch careless analysts
give up before they even read them.

Most people who poke around this directory
will quit before they open the first one.

— KAEL

So there is not any credentials. So I need to see the content in the file named 'file name'. 

cat 'file name'

docs.title: Password:
docs.text:
I got the flag which is,

D4shIsN0tAFl4g

Next Ghost L2

docs.title: SSH access:
docs.text:
Host: 204.168.229.209 Port: 2222 User: ghost2 Password: D4shIsN0tAFl4g

When I opened the machine as soon as possible I used ls -a to see all the files and directories in the machine. There I seen many files and directory in it, few notables are .memo, .profile, and a directory investigation. So I used cat command in .memo and .profile. In .profile file I don't got any usefull information. But in .memo I got some message.

docs.title: .memo file:
docs.text:
NOTE TO SELF — KAEL

The work happens off the main paths.
Compartmentalization is the only real opsec.
If it's in plain sight, it's not worth finding.

docs.title: Investigation directory:
docs.text: So I moved to investigation directory and used ls -a to see all the files and directories in it. I found two files namely report.txt, summary.txt and a directory .leads. I used cat command for those two file and got these,

docs.title: Content of report.txt:
docs.text:
INCIDENT REPORT — Q1 2026
Status: Classified
Prepared by: KAEL

Summary: Unauthorized access detected on segment C.
Response: Ongoing. Active leads compartmentalized.

Full details filed separately.

So I got some messages from report.txt. So now I used cat command in summary.txt.

docs.title: Content of summary.txt:
docs.text:
OPERATIONAL SUMMARY
===================
Operation: GHOST WATCH
Status: Active

All active source files have been compartmentalized
and moved to a separate location.

This document contains no credentials.

docs.title: .leads directory:
docs.text:
In this file also I don't got the credentials. So I moved to .leads directory and used ls -a to see all the files and directories in it. I found some files namely .source_alpha, .source_beta, .source_omega. So I need to use cat command to all the files in this directory. If I use one by one it may consume time. So I used this,

cat .*

docs.title: Content of the files:
docs.text:
7a4e91c63d2f
bb50d8e4a11c
H1dd3nInSh4dow

docs.title: Password:
docs.text:
H1dd3nInSh4dow

SO I got the password let's move to next level.

Ghost L3

docs.title: SSH access:
docs.text:
Host: 204.168.229.209 Port: 2222 User: ghost3 Password: H1dd3nInSh4dow

When I Logged in to the machine I seen only one notable file namely map.txt. SO I used cat command to it and found some useful information.

docs.title: Content of map.txt:
docs.text:
KAEL'S STORAGE LAYOUT
=====================
Recovered from workstation. Partially redacted.

  /var/intel/public/   — world readable
  /var/intel/ops/      — restricted
  /var/intel/archive/  — root only

Access follows the group scheme. The kernel will
tell you what you are, if you ask it.

— KAEL

They says to access some directories namely public, ops, archive in /var/intel/. So I used cd command to go to those directory.

docs.title: Public directory:
docs.text:
First I moved to public directory and used ls -a to see all the files and directories in it.

cd /var/intel/public/

ls -a
Here I found only one text file namely report_q1.txt and I used cat command to it. And got some information.

OPEN INTELLIGENCE REPORT — Q1 2026
Classification: Public

This document contains no operational credentials.
For restricted files, consult your access group.

I don't got the flag so next I move to archive directory.

docs.title: archive directory:
docs.text:
I used te given commad to move to archive directory,

cd /var/intel/archive/
I got a message Permission denied So I not a root user. So I decided to move to ops directory and want to see all the file in it.

docs.title: ops directory:
docs.text:
I used the below command to move to the ops directory and used ls -a to see all the files and directories in it.

cd /var/intel/ops

ls -a

So I found some files namely operative_list.txt, access_codes.dat. I used cat command to all the files. But I got only information from access_codes.dat.

From operative_list.txt

INDEX OF ACTIVE OPERATIONS
Classification: Analyst Only

See access_codes.dat for current credentials.

Now From access_codes.dat

P3rm1ss10ns_M4tt3r

docs.title: Password:
docs.text:
So got the password in the file.

P3rm1ss10ns_M4tt3r

Next ghost L4
